data.title: FX And U.S. Election Cheat Sheet
data.description:
By Francesco Pesole, Frantisek Taborsky & Chris Turner

This article is part of our US Election coverage and focuses on near-term dynamics. We discuss election scenarios and medium-term currency market implications in detail in “US election guide for the FX market”.

The latest polls suggest the upcoming US election is a close call, but financial and betting markets have recently swung more in favour of a Donald Trump win. We will walk you through the numbers in key battleground states, discuss how we see market positioning in the two weeks leading to the election, and explore the potential initial reactions in FX.

The 2024 US swing states cheat sheet

Battleground numbers

The table above summarises what polls and betting markets are telling us about the upcoming Presidential election for seven battlegrounds (or “swing”) states and the national outlook. According to the latest poll aggregates, 226 electoral college votes are either solidly or leaning Democrat, while 219 are for the Republicans. The seven battleground states listed are closely contested, with leads within the statistical margin of error. To reach the 270 electoral college win threshold, Harris needs to secure 44 of the 73 available swing state votes; Trump needs 51 – assuming all lock/lean states don’t flip.

"Trump is marginally ahead in the swing states"

If the latest poll averages (third column) prove correct, Trump wins the election with Republican lock/lean votes (219) + Arizona (11) + Georgia (16) + North Carolina (16) + Pennsylvania (19) = 281 electoral college votes. Harris would need to win all states where she is already ahead in the polls (including Michigan, Nevada and Wisconsin) plus another 13 electoral college votes from the swing states where Trump is currently leading. That means securing Arizona (11) alone wouldn’t be enough, and Harris would need to win either Georgia (16), North Carolina (16) or Pennsylvania (19), with the latter widely seen as the state that can tip the balance.

Polls versus bets

While there is no simple market measure of the Harris/Trump implied probability, betting markets are often taken as a benchmark. In the table above, we see that the traditional bookmaker (Betfair) odds highly favour Trump. We also looked at the CFTC-regulated portal Kalshi, where it is possible to buy/sell the equivalent of binary options on either candidate.

Betting markets have given Trump a better chance than the polls

Kalshi’s election winner market only started in October, so the chart above uses data from PredictIt, an analogue election-betting website. Both Kalshi and PredictIt implied probability of a win has generally been leaning more in favour of Trump relative to what poll-based simulations were suggesting. Remember that these portals work similarly to stock markets, where the price is determined by buying and selling volume.

The rise in bets on Trump in such markets is probably a reflection of both polls and some hedging, considering a Trump win is seen as the more impactful event for markets. Incidentally, in both 2016 and 2020, the Republicans fared markedly better than polls and betting markets had anticipated, and that can also explain why betting markets have been favouring Trump this time.

FX implied volatility to rise into the vote

In the last two US presidential elections, the cost of FX hedging increased significantly in the two weeks leading up to the vote. One way to measure this is the ratio of one-month implied volatility to one-month historical volatility. A ratio above 1.0 suggests that markets anticipate larger spot movements in the upcoming month compared to the previous 30 days.

Rise in FX implied volatility may have only just started

As you can see above, the implied/historical volatility ratio increased markedly in the 14 days preceding the 2020 and 2016 election days for G10 dollar crosses. We expect a similar dynamic this time, especially considering the latest polls are narrowly favouring Trump, whose win can generate larger volatility across the currency market. Since that hedging demand should mostly be related to protection for a Trump-led dollar rally, we think the balance of risks remains skewed to a stronger USD into the vote.

"FX liquidity can dry up close to the vote"

In particular, we remain concerned that some de-risking in the FX market can lead to poorer liquidity conditions. The Norwegian krone is often a good indicator of such conditions, given it is the least liquid G10 currency. Despite good fundamentals, we suspect EUR/NOK can trade back above 12.0 before the US election.

FX market isn't fully pricing in Trump

Since mid-October, markets have gradually priced in more Trump risk, mainly through higher US rates, pressure on emerging market currencies and some USD strength. This means that a potential “relief” rally following a Harris win can now be larger and hit the dollar harder.

That said, the FX market is not fully pricing in a Trump victory. The dollar’s strength is still mostly a function of stronger US data, and EUR/USD (currently at 1.082) is trading less than 1% below its short-term fair value. An undervaluation of at least 2% (the 1.5 standard deviation) would be needed to conclude there is a Trump-related risk premium embedded in the pair.

"EUR/USD is not embedding a Trump risk premium"

Over the recent period, EM currencies have sold off on the strong dollar but have not – yet – substantially underperformed G10 currencies. This may change. Most vulnerable could be CEE currencies, which have double exposure through EUR crosses and large export openness, translating into sensitivity to potential changes in global trade in the case of a Trump victory.

At the moment, HUF seems the most exposed within the region, where the central bank does not have many options to defend the currency and the market has already outpriced any rate cuts over the last two weeks. On the other hand, the CZK and PLN seem more defensive and may also benefit from a possible relief in case of a Harris victory.

Elsewhere, things could have been worse for the Latin and Asian currencies were it not for recent Chinese stimulus measures. Yet both blocs still look vulnerable to further losses under a full Trump 2.0.

FX market reaction to Trump's 2016 win

Gauging the initial FX impact

The 2020 Presidential election was somewhat unique, and results were significantly delayed due to the very high number of mail-in ballots and the Republicans contesting the count in some states. In the table at the top of this article, we summarised the point at which the key swing states were called by the Associated Press in the past three elections. It’s worth noting that Biden’s win was not officially called until the Saturday after the vote (so four days after).

There is probably a greater risk of a delay in the count and official results for this election compared to any other election before 2020, and news agencies may well be more careful in calling a state or the Presidency than in previous instances. Most swing states are on the East Coast, where polls close between 7PM and 8PM ET, but the high volume of mail-in ballots can cause delays. The preliminary results in Pennsylvania can have one of the deepest market impacts as this is seen as a must-win state for Harris, but local regulation allows mail-in votes to be counted only on Election Day, which can lead to a lengthy count.

Anyway, there is a good possibility the FX market will “call” the winner already on the night between 5 November and 6 November. We expect the initial reaction to mostly entail protectionism-related trades. This means the wider swings can be seen in AUD and NZD in the G10 space; in EM, Asian currencies and MXN will be particularly sensitive.

Which currencies are most exposed to Trump tariffs?

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Blackstone: Time To Take Profits
data.description:
Blackstone Inc. (NYSE:BX) had better-than-expected results for the third quarter last week as its profits grew YoY and its asset management business is firing on all cylinders.

Blackstone’s assets under management reached $1.1 trillion, and investors received the quarterly earnings release very well, sending the stock to new highs.

Though Blackstone’s 3Q24 earnings were great, and the asset manager is enjoying some decent momentum in terms of asset inflows, I don’t think the valuation makes much sense here with a distributable earnings multiple of 42x.

Consequently, I don’t see a particularly compelling risk/reward relationship and have decided to take profits.

My Rating History

My last stock classification on Blackstone was Hold. Growing assets under management and a potential investment opportunity in distressed real estate justified a hold classification at that particular moment in time, in my view, though I was already cautious at the time about the asset manager’s valuation. Blackstone’s third quarter earnings led to a new stock high on Friday, which is when I closed my position in the asset manager.

Growth In Assets Under Management And Distributable Earnings

Blackstone produced $1.4 billion in distributable profits in the third quarter, up 1% YoY. The asset manager had a particularly good quarter in terms of its management and advisory business, which produced 8% higher earnings compared to the year ago period. In total, this segment contributed $1.79 billion in earnings in the third quarter. Weaker performance was booked in the fee-related performance business, which is where profits fell 4% YoY.

Blackstone did particularly well in the third quarter in terms of reeling in new assets. Total assets under management went up 10% YoY to $1.11 trillion, while fee-earning assets under management climbed 12% YoY to $820.5 billion.

Inflows were strong, particularly in credit & insurance as well as in private equity, where the asset management company gathered $21.4 billion and $10.2 billion in assets, respectively. Strong asset collection is an indicator of platform strength and could point to growing profits for the asset manager in the future.

Taking into account that Blackstone’s valuation is already quite stretched based on distributable earnings, I think that this might actually be a good time to lock in profits for investors.

Dividend Pay-Out Metrics

Blackstone earned $1.01 per share in distributable earnings in the third quarter, up 7% YoY on growth in the asset management and advisory segment primarily.

The asset manager paid out $0.86 per share in dividends in the third quarter, which equates to an 85% dividend pay-out ratio. This is equal to the amount of distributable earnings the investment company paid out in the last twelve months as well.

Blackstone’s dividend here is very well-covered by distributable earnings and while I myself was invested in part because of the dividend, the dividend yield alone is not a sufficiently good reason to pay a sky-high premium for the asset management company.

With Blackstone’s stock price skyrocketing, the company’s dividend yield has compressed down to just 2%. The yield stood at 4.6% when I first presented Blackstone as an investment opportunity back in February 10, 2023.

Unsustainable Valuation

I have been cautious on Blackstone for valuation reasons before, but the present multiple likely implies negative return potential in the future, in my view. Based on run-rate 3Q24 distributable earnings, Blackstone is selling for a profit multiple of 43x, which seems excessively high.

I estimated in my earlier presentation about the asset manager that Blackstone could earn $4.10-4.15 per share this year in distributable earnings. Based on this range, the profit multiple slips to 42x, which is still excessively high.

KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR), for which I estimated full-year profit potential of $3.80-4.00 per share in 2024, is selling for $139.81 at the time of writing, which reflects back to us a 36x leading (2024) distributable earnings multiple.

Both asset managers are quite expensive at this point, which makes buying them at this point kind of uncompelling.

Since Blackstone is now also much more expensive than KKR, I have decided to sell my BX stock on Friday and lock in all my profits.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Not Turn Out In My Favor

Blackstone reported robust results for 3Q24 that showed a broad acceleration in the company’s asset management business. Institutions are still very comfortable putting money into Blackstone’s asset management segment, which could propel the stock price higher, so there is definitely the risk of missing out on more upside.

With that said, I do have problems with the valuation multiple here and asset management businesses also tend to be cyclical, meaning they perform well when the stock market is performing well also.

Taking into account the cyclical component inherent in Blackstone’s asset management business, I see even much less reason to own the stock moving forward.

On the flipside, in case the stock market reaches new highs and investors are comfortable putting money into Blackstone’s funds, then there is a possibility for Blackstone to report strong asset and earnings growth which may make the stock cheaper on a distributable profit basis.

My Conclusion

The third quarter was clearly a good one for Blackstone from a business perspective: Profits grew, the asset manager is witnessing asset inflows across its businesses and distributable earnings, the ultimate measure of success, were up as well.

Blackstone’s distribution earnings rose 7% per share YoY, and the company did well in terms of growing its assets under management.

Taking into account that Blackstone’s stock has performed very well and that it now sells for more than 42x estimated 2024 distributable earnings, I think the risk/reward relationship is not that favorable. The dividend yield also contracted to just 2%, down from 4.6% when I first recommended Blackstone.

data.title: Nordea: Profitability Slowly Normalizing, But Shares Cheap At 7.3x Earnings
data.description:
Shares of pan-Nordic banking giant Nordea (OTCQX:NRDBY)(OTCQX:NBNKF) have lagged wider European financials (EUFN) over the past year, albeit having still returned ~20% thanks to the boost from higher interest rates.

I first covered Nordea back in April, rating it a "Buy" as the bank's mid/high teens return on equity ("ROE") more than offset the stock's modest premium to book value. These shares have been a fairly disappointing performer since then, again lagging broader European financials by a double-digit margin.

Recent financial results indicate that Nordea is still over-earning relative to management's ROE target, but not by enough to leave the stock expensive when profitability normalizes. I remain bullish on the bank and keep my previous rating in place.

Q3 Overview

In the company's third quarter, Nordea reported net income of €1.27 billion, down ~6% year-on-year and ~3% quarter-on-quarter. EPS of €0.36 (~$0.39 per ADS) was slightly more resilient, falling ~5% year-on-year as buybacks provided a modest tailwind.

While net income is falling, this is coming off a high base, as the steep rise in interest rates significantly widened Nordea's net interest margin ("NIM") in 2022 and 2023. At the same time, credit quality has also remained very resilient, and this combination has basically led to ROE remaining near cycle-highs. Given that, some degree of earnings normalization is to be expected, so declining net income is not concerning as such.

Starting with interest income trends, Nordea's NIM was 177bps last quarter, flat year-on-year but down around 6bps sequentially as interest rate cuts weigh on deposit margins. Again, this decline basically represents a base effect more than anything else, and NIM remains ~60bps higher than it was before European policy rates started to rise in 2022:

At the same time, the current interest rate environment hasn't yet spurred a meaningful increase in demand for credit. Nordea's retail and business/corporate lending balances were basically static last quarter, although deposit balances did show better performance, rising by ~4%. With margins contracting slightly and volumes still subdued, net interest income ("NII") was also fairly weak, falling ~1% year-on-year to €1.88 billion.

Elsewhere, fee income was stronger, increasing ~4% year-on-year to €774 million. Nordea has a relatively significant asset management ("AM") business, and AM fees constitute its largest source of non-interest income (and ~15% of total income). Thanks to positive flows and strong markets, total AUM rose ~15% year-on-year to €412.4 billion last quarter, while AM fee income rose ~5% to €432 million. Business lines adjacent to its core banking business also reported growth, with payments and card income rising ~8% year-on-year to €150 million as customer activity picked up.

Costs were a bit of a mixed bag. At €1.33 billion, operating expenses were up around 11% year-on-year and 4% sequentially in Q3, outpacing both revenue and wider inflation. This drove a decline in pre-provision operating income, which fell 4% year-on-year to €1.69 billion. Operating expenses were somewhat muddied by certain one-offs (namely M&A integration expenses and a U.S. compliance fine), and "core" expense growth was better, increasing by ~1% year-on-year. Nordic banks in general are well-known for being efficient, and Nordea's cost/income ratio was still under 45% in Q3 even including these one-offs.

In contrast, loan loss charges continue to come in surprisingly light, padding earnings. Credit charges were just €51 million last quarter, or roughly 6bps of total loans, boosted by a modest release of management's macro overlays. Stage 3 loans (i.e. non-performing) did tick up 10bps sequentially to 105bps, and while this will be something to monitor going forward, the NPL rate remains at low levels relative to historical levels. Since credit charges remain below management's 10bps through-the-cycle target, this line will likely be a modest headwind to earnings/profitability in the future.

Looking at particular areas of investor concern, commercial real estate is not as significant at Nordea compared to other major Scandinavian banks, though at ~10% of total loans it is still meaningful. Average loan-to-value ratios are around 50% in all of Nordea's major markets, so the bank has a significant cushion even if property prices were to fall substantially.

Turning to the balance sheet, Nordea remains very well capitalized, with its CET1 ending last quarter at 15.8%. While down from over 17% at initial coverage, model changes were expected to drive an increase in risk-weighted assets, so this decline was anticipated. More importantly, the current CET1 remains around 80bps ahead of management's 15% goal, implying around €1.2 billion in surplus capital.

Shares Can Still Be Cheap

Looking ahead, management expects NII to trough next year or possibly in 2026, as deposit margins eventually stabilize and volume growth returns to modest positive territory.

The quicker we see the rate cuts, the sooner we'll see the trough. And that could be in '25, given the sort of spillover effect of rate cuts, may go over into 2026.

Ian Smith, Nordea Group Chief Financial Officer

As mentioned above, earnings normalization is not a problem as such. Nordea's ROE was still 16.8% last quarter and 17.5% year-to-date. With €8.63 in average year-to-date book value per share ("BVPS")(~$9.34 per ADS), the current €10.94 share price (~$11.85 per ADS) only puts Nordea on a roughly 7.3x multiple of YTD earnings (i.e. a ~1.27x BVPS multiple divided by a ~17.5% ROE). Said in plainer English, the market is already pricing in lower future earnings.

Given that, the key questions are, one, where Nordea's profitability will settle across the cycle, and two, how expensive the stock is relative to this. Management's longer-term ROE target is ~15%, which would be a decline compared to 2023 (~16.9%) and YTD 2024 (~17.5%), but materially above the ~10% ROE that the bank averaged before interest rates started rising in 2022. Given the forward curve still points to modestly positive Eurozone rates at the end of the easing cycle, a structural step-up in profitability does not strike me as an unreasonable assumption.

Assuming a ~15% ROE, Nordea stock would trade for ~8.1x mid-cycle earnings based on its Q3 BVPS multiple (i.e. 1.22x divided by 15%). Theoretically that means the shares would yield over 12% if management simply distributed 100% of net income to shareholders. That also means the stock could still be quite cheap even if earnings do fall modestly.

Nordea will probably retain some of its earnings to fund growth. The bank's operating footprint is quite evenly spread between the four major Nordic countries. Given its size and current market shares, long-term growth in line with nominal GDP is probably a fair assumption – and realistically that will be somewhere around 2% based on past figures for these countries.

Assuming a ~15% ROE, Nordea would only need to retain around 15% of its through-the-cycle earnings to fund this (a 15% ROE multiplied by 15% retained income implies roughly 2% growth), leaving the rest available for dividends and buybacks. Note that this isn't entirely theoretical - based on a ~15% ROE management does intend to pay out most of its net income to investors in 2024 and 2025:

That gives the shares a roughly ~12-13% long-term total returns potential even on lower mid-cycle earnings, with that consisting of ~10-11% from capital returns (~12.3% earnings yield multiplied by ~85%) plus approximately 2% from underlying growth.

Conclusion

Nordea's net income is falling, albeit this is normal as the macro environment normalizes from peak-cycle conditions. More importantly, this is more than reflected in the stock's current valuation, with Nordea trading for ~7.3x year-to-date 2024 earnings and ~8.1x potential mid-cycle earnings based on management's 15% ROE target. With even the latter figure implying attractive double-digit returns potential, Nordea shares have a reasonable margin of safety in the event that profitability undershoots this. I remain bullish and leave my initial "Buy" rating unchanged.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Don't Count Out The Combustibles
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Philip Morris International sees strong ZYN volumes. (0:15) GM internal combustion strength counters China weakness. (1:18) IMF lowers 2025 global growth outlook. (3:17)

Our top story so far. Don’t count out cigarettes and gasoline just yet.

Tobacco and automobiles—two things that helped build America—saw the biggest recognition in a massive day of earnings reports.

Philip Morris International (PMI) and General Motors (NYSE:GM) are the top gainers in the S&P 500 following their results, both up more than 8%.

Philip Morris revenue rose 8.4% year-over-year during the quarter to $9.91 billion. On an organic basis, revenue jumped 11.6%. EPS came in at $1.91 vs. $1.56 consensus and $1.67 a year ago.

CEO Jacek Olczak said the numbers reflect “excellent momentum across all regions and categories, with a reacceleration in IQOS adjusted in-market sales growth, strong ZYN volumes, and resilient combustible performance."

Looking ahead, the company predicts full-year adjusted EPS of $6.45 to $6.51 (midpoint $6.48) vs. a prior outlook for $6.33 to $6.45 and $6.41 consensus. The company sees net revenue growth of around 9.5% on an organic basis.

GM was fueled by the strength of its internal combustion engine business, which accounts for over 80% of its U.S. business.

That overcame struggles in China, where market share shrank to 6.5% from 8.3% a year ago, resulting in a loss of $137 million. CFO Paul Jacobson attributes the profitability challenge in China to a “sizable inventory build” that necessitated incentive pricing.

For the remainder of 2024, GM again raised its bottom-line profit forecast to $14 billion to $15 billion from the previous forecast of $13 billion to $15 billion. Adjusted automotive free cash flow is now expected to be between $12.5 billion and $13.5 billion from $9.5 billion to $11.5 billion in the company’s previous forecast.

Among other major earnings reports, GE Aerospace (GE) fell after the maker of jet engines said revenue grew 6% from a year earlier to $8.9 billion, shy of the consensus of $9.38 billion.

Verizon (VZ) was also under pressure as it reported a marginal bottom line beat and a slight topline miss. It did reaffirm previous annual guidance, though.

Seeking Alpha analyst Michael Dion said: “On the business side, I continue to be disappointed as management continues to pitch this as the growth business, but it never gets any traction. In a world of cybersecurity, AI, and 5G, Verizon has no excuse to be underperforming in this space."

Lockheed Martin (LMT) reported disappointing sales growth. Sales edged up 1% from a year earlier to $17.1 billion, less than the average estimate of $17.37 billion.

And Genuine Parts (GPC) plunged nearly 20% as the company’s disappointing Q3 results coupled with significantly lowered guidance for FY24 chased shares to their lowest level since March 2022. For the full year, the company now expects revenue growth of just 1% to 2% versus an earlier forecast of 1% to 3% growth, while earnings are now expected to be between $8.00 and $8.20 per share from initial guidance of $9.30 to $9.50 per share.

On the macro front, the data calendar is pretty much empty, but the International Monetary Fund slightly lowered its global economic outlook for 2025 and kept its 2024 projection unchanged from its July estimates, masking numerous under-the-hood revisions.

While inflation has cooled, risks to the global outlook "are tilted to the downside amid elevated policy uncertainty," the IMF said. Geopolitical tensions could trigger new spikes in commodity prices, China's property sector contraction could spill over, and protectionist policies could "exacerbate trade tensions, reduce market efficiency, and further disrupt supply chains."

For 2024, the IMF made the biggest upward revisions for Brazil, Spain, Russia, and the U.K. On the flip side, Mexico and Japan saw the biggest downward revisions.

U.S. output is projected to rise 2.8% in 2024, up 0.2 pp from July, and 2.2% in 2025, up 0.3 pp.

In other news of note, Walmart (WMT) is adding a new service that has the potential to be disruptive. It will begin to deliver prescriptions to households in the U.S. in as little as 30 minutes. The service will see customers have the ability to add prescriptions and medication refills to their orders for groceries and other products. The service will be free for customers of Walmart+.

Walmart aims to have prescription delivery available in 49 states by the end of January, with coverage of more than 86% of U.S. households.

In a recent survey, Walmart customers were asked how the company could better serve them. Fifty-five percent expressed a desire to have their prescriptions delivered along with their groceries and other items they need in a single online order.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Goldman Sachs predicts a better environment for income investors than what’s priced in by the market.

Strategist David Kostin forecast S&P 500 (SP500) dividend per share of $80 in 2025, which is 5% above market pricing at $76. He predicts dividends will grow by 7% this year and another 7% next year.

“Despite weak EPS growth in 2023, mega-cap tech dividend initiations have supported DPS (dividend per share) growth in 2024,” Kostin said.

“Futures contracts ultimately pay out based on the level of realized DPS, with considerable volatility along the way. Investors who can tolerate that volatility should be rewarded as futures market pricing converges towards our fundamental forecasts,” he added.

Among the names in Goldman’s Dividend Yield and Growth Basket are Interpublic Group (IPG), eBay (EBAY), Molson Coors (TAP), Devon Energy (DVN), Wells Fargo (WFC), Cigna (CI), Fastenal (FAST), IBM (IBM), LyondellBasell (LYB), SBA Communications (SBAC), and WEC Energy (WEC).

data.title: BriaCell Therapeutics: High-Potential In Phase 3 Metastatic Breast Cancer, But Financing Concerns
data.description:
Thesis

I have covered BriaCell Therapeutics (NASDAQ:BCTX) (NASDAQ:BCTXW) (TSX:BCT:CA) before in January 2023, and I am reissuing a Buy recommendation based on recent news and the latest progress.

The company is currently in a Phase 3 trial with Bria-IMT for metastatic breast cancer, with a potential interim analysis in the second half of 2025. A potential BLA submission could then follow in the first half of 2026. Bria-OTS is an earlier-stage asset with high potential in a Phase 1/2 trial.

Although the company's progression from a Phase 2 trial into a Phase 3 trial on the basis of consistent data has been impressive, and the thesis is still intact, the stock has come down strong since my previous coverage.

The company's market cap is close to $30 million at the time of writing. In case of success, the company counts on a $1-$5 billion market to open up in case of approval. My valuation exercise leads me to possible valuations in between $1 and $2 billion.

However, financing and shareholder dilution seem to be the issue here. The company has done repeated offerings over the past months, which allow it to continue operations, and may continue to do so going forward.

From that perspective, chances are reasonable that the stock rerates considerably going into the potentially pivotal year 2025. Right now seems like a good time to cover the stock, with a big catalyst less than twelve months away.

Company

BriaCell Therapeutics (BCTX) (BCTXW) is a Canadian biotech company focused on developing treatments against various solid tumors, by using replication-incompetent and engineered cell lines against tumors. The market performance of this stock over the past five years has been outright abominable.

Its lead therapy candidate is Bria-IMT, and its second therapy candidate is Bria-OTS. BriaCell has received FDA fast track designation for Bria-IMT for the treatment of metastatic breast cancer.

Bria-IMT and Bria-OTS are different to many other therapies because they stimulate the patient's innate immune system by way of an irradiated cancer cell line which they have subsequently engineered.

Bria-IMT is a late-stage asset in Phase 3 trials, and Bria-OTS had its Phase 1/2 trial recently initiated.

The FDA granted Bria-IMT fast-track designation on the basis of its tolerability profile and overall survival data, improving that reported in literature by 50% or more.

Since my last coverage, the company is now focusing on getting its lead candidate to market in breast cancer, instead of initiating trials in a variety of solid tumors. This is its current development and catalyst timeline.

This is the company's full pipeline.

Bria-IMT

Bria-IMT is a replication-incompetent allogeneic cell line derived from a breast cancer cell. It expresses HLA class I and II molecules, which may make patients with matching HLA classes more susceptible to successful treatment. The goal of Bria-IMT is to let it be recognized by the body's immune system, and to boost the immune response by secreting GM-CSF.

The HLA system encodes cell surface molecules that present antigens for T-cells. People have different HLA types. Bria-IMT presents HLA class I and II molecules, and better responses have been seen in patients with matching HLA types.

Good safety profile

Several tests have now been performed with BriaCell-IMT, and in each of them, no significant adverse events have been noted, apart from injection site irritation. The therapy candidate has a perfect tolerability profile.

Consistent trial results show a near twofold increase in overall survival

Across trials so far, Bria-IMT has shown consistency in prolonging overall survival in patients. On average, that consistency leads to about a double in overall survival and median progression-free survival compared to historical controls, if patients are treated with the Phase 3 formulation, and much higher clinical benefits seen across cancer types. Otherwise, there is still a benefit compared to historical controls, but less. This was a trial in heavily pretreated patients who had failed more than 3 prior therapy attempts, meaning these patients are very end-stage.

BriaCell's Phase 2 trial tested patients on Bria-IMT combined with two checkpoint inhibitors, pembrolizumab and retifanlimab, and did so with and without IFN-γ. The dosing without IFN-γ produced better results and showed consistent results in different types of breast cancer.

In summary, with the Phase 3 dosing regimen, overall survival of patients on Bria-IMT with checkpoint inhibitors without IFN-γ was 15.6 months compared to 5.9-9.8 months, meaning about 7.85 on average, for historical controls.

Median progression-free survival was 3.9 months compared to 1.6-2.5, meaning 2.05 on average, in the literature. In the slide below, contrary to the one above, orange indicates the group on the Phase 3 formulation.

Furthermore, clinical benefit was seen in 55% of evaluable patients across different types of breast cancer, compared to only 7-10% as mentioned in literature.

The April 2024 press release even mentioned progression-free survival of 4.2 months in ADC resistant patients receiving the Phase 3 formulation and a clinical benefit rate of 56%.

On October 22, 2024, the company reported that 55% of patients remained alive one year after initiating therapy, and several patients had a survival of over 2 years.

The aforementioned Phase 2 trial results were also entirely consistent with previous trials, in which Bria-IMT had each time shown superiority to existing therapies. I refer to my previous coverage in that respect.

Already in December 2021, BriaCell had presented data showing for patients on checkpoint inhibitors from three different trials in patients who had failed on average 5 to 6 prior regimens. The data showed an overall survival of 12 months, mPFS of 4.2 months and DCR of 33%.

The combination with checkpoint inhibitors appeared superior to monotherapy, and even better results were seen in HLA-matching patients.

The combination therapy with checkpoint inhibitors makes sense, as Bria-IMT activates the immune system while checkpoint inhibitors neutralize immune suppression.

Interestingly, also, efficacy of Bria-IMT is even seen in intracranial tumors, showing efficacy of Bria-IMT across the blood-brain-barrier. the company has reported on several occasions on the complete resolution of an 'eye-bulging' tumor in a patient who had failed 8 prior regimens, the ongoing regression of an intracranial tumor in another patient, and an 83% intracranial objective response rate.

The ongoing Phase 3 trial

BriaCell has a pivotal Phase 3 trial underway in metastatic breast cancer which, in case of positive results, could result in full approval. This trial was initiated in October 2023.

This trial combines Bria-IMT with a checkpoint inhibitor. This is the design of that pivotal study, with overall survival as its primary endpoint.

The study will enroll a total of 354 patients. The pivotal part of the study compares Bria-IMT with a checkpoint inhibitor as the physician's treatment of choice, but the trial also has a Bria-IMT alone arm.

The last reported update on this trial is that it has 35 active sites, with additional sites in various stages of startup, and that enrollment is expected to be completed mid-2025.

An interim readout which could give way to full approval is scheduled for mid-2025. That readout will be made on the basis of 144 occurrences of death - 144 'events' - which can then allow comparison between overall survival in any of the Bria-IMT regimens and overall survival when treated by the physician's choice.

As part of Bria-IMT's Fast Track Designation program, the FDA has also authorized an expanded access policy for metastatic breast cancer patients outside of the scope of the Phase 3 clinical trial.

Bria-OTS trial in breast cancer underway, and prostate cancer start imminent

As mentioned above, Bria-IMT performs better in HLA-matching patients, and Bria-OTS has been developed exactly in line with that understanding.

Bria-OTS is an off-the-shelf therapy which is essentially an HLA-matching version of Bria-IMT, with different HLA-subtypes for a personalized treatment. Bria-OTS, among other things, works by activating natural killer cells to counter cancer immune escape caused by the loss of human leukocyte antigens (HLA).

Bria-OTS has been given different names depending on the type of cancer that is being targeted. Bria-BRES+, Bria-PROS+, Bria-LUNG+ and Bria-MEL+ are the names for breast cancer, prostate cancer, lung cancer and melanoma respectively.

A Phase 1/2a study is underway in advanced metastatic breast cancer since May 2024, both as monotherapy and in combination with PD-1 inhibitor tislelizumab.

In September 2024, BriaCell announced a positive pre-IND meeting with the FDA for a trial initiation in prostate cancer, during which the FDA has waived the animal toxicology and animal pharmacokinetic (PK) studies requirement for opening the IND. The hope is to start a Phase 1/2 trial for prostate cancer in the first half of 2025.

Both trials should deliver continuous patient data over the course of the years 2025 and 2026, and the company hopes to launch additional trials in melanoma and lung cancer over that same period.

Valuation

At the time of writing, BriaCell has a market cap slightly below $30 million.

In light of Bria-IMT's Phase 3 trial and Bria-OTS's Phase 1/2 in metastatic breast cancer, I believe one can focus on breast cancer for now.

The global breast cancer market size is almost $32 billion, and grows with a CAGR of about 10%, with 264,000 cases being diagnosed in women in the US each year. About 40,000 deaths from breast cancer are reported each year. About 150,000 women in the U.S. alone develop breast cancer each year, of which about 45% will receive third line therapy, which would mean there are about 68,000 patients eligible for treatment with Bria-IMT. Recently approved breast cancer treatments cost $16,000 per month on average. Approval could be as early as 2026.

Assuming a treatment of one year, that could mean $160,000 in revenue per patient. Peak market penetration of 5,000-10,000 patients per year would in such case generate annual sales revenue of in between $800 million and $1.6 billion. The median EV/revenue multiple for biotech companies in 2023 was close to 13. But even with a 5x multiple on $500 million in peak sales revenue, discounted back for three years at a 20% discount rate, the company should still have a discounted value of $1 billion. With higher potential peak sales, the market cap should logically increase proportionally. A peak sales assumption of $1 billion could lead to a $2 billion valuation.

Below is an overview of marketed therapies on the basis of their objective response rates and progression-free survival, showing that these therapies generated between $1.1 and $25 billion in revenue in 2023.

There is currently only one analyst covering BriaCell, Emily Bodnar of H.C. Wainwright, with a current price target of $15, which represents a 1,820% upside and represents about $500 million. In September 2024, that price target was still $18.

Logically, in case of success, the stock could be revalued even higher.

However, the above valuation analysis does not include the financing situation BriaCell is facing. That situation seems to be the issue keeping the stock from attracting more investors, as I will explain below.

Finances

According to the latest quarterly report, BriaCell had only $0.9 million in cash at the end of April 2024 as compared to $21 million at the end of July 2023. At that time, about 15 million shares had been issued, similar to the number of shares that were issued in the framework of my previous coverage.

Cash burn was $27.3 million in 2023, which would be about $2.25 million per month.

The company has done several offerings in May, September and October of 2024 for a total of $18.5 million. These three financings have added 2,402,935, 12,325,000 and 5,128,500 common shares respectively, or a total of 19,856,435 million shares.

At the cash burn rate of 2023, the company may have already burnt through about $13.5 million of its financings by the end of October 2024, leaving only $5 million remaining which could cover another two months of operations.

The above-mentioned financings also included warrants to purchase up to an aggregate of 2,402,935 common shares at $2.11 per share, exercisable as of November 2024, and 5,128,500 warrants at a $0.85 per share exercise price exercisable immediately. Once the share price goes above $0.85, I assume these warrants could start being exercised.

As a result, shareholder dilution has been massive recently and may continue to be massive going forward.

According to the latest corporate presentation for autumn 2024 made in October 2024, BriaCell had 35.8 million shares outstanding, 2.1 million options with a weighted average exercise price of $6.16 and 16.6 million warrants with a weighted average exercise price of $3.45.

BriaCell also has an AI collaboration with ImmunoPrecise Antibodies (IPA) following which it would need to pay $500,000 in case of successful anti-cancer antibody discovery. I actually own shares of IPA, but am not sure whether this collaboration has already amounted to something. If it did, then some of the above financing would need to go to IPA.

Risks

BriaCell has not received approval for its treatments yet. The competition in the oncology space is high, and good results from a competitor may lower expectations for success and commercialization for Bria-IMT or Bria-OTS.

The trial can also not meet its endpoints, in which case a more difficult path to approval would await the company, and other regulatory hurdles could occur along the way.

The company is ramping up trials, which may mean its cash burn accelerates.

The stock is trading under a dollar for the moment, but apparently has not received a warning from the SEC yet in that respect. If it does, it has six months to fix that situation, although that period can often be prolonged. The company can also elect to do a reverse-stock-split to become more attractive to investors.

Finally, BriaCell's stock can be highly volatile. In September 2024, it rocketed 150% on Positive Phase 2 data. At the time of writing, the stock is up 15% premarket on a positive further update from the Phase 2 trial.

Conclusion

Bria-IMT has consistently presented excellent results across different subtypes of metastatic breast cancer, doubling overall survival and median progression-free survival compared to literature. The latest news, reported on October 22, 2024, is that 55% of patients remained alive one year after initiating therapy, and several patients had survival of over 2 years. This data is in line with previous therapies in late-stage terminally ill breast cancer patients.

Data from the Phase 3 trial is scheduled to be reported in the second half of 2025. That makes the upcoming months particularly interesting.

The company now also has a trial with Bria-OTS ongoing, entitled Bria-BRES+, and hopes to initiate a trial with Bria-OTS in prostate cancer in the first half of 2025. Data from these trials could come in over the course of 2025 and 2026.

If BriaCell Therapeutics were successful in its Phase 3 trial in metastatic breast cancer, a market with $1-$5 million in revenue potential could open up. My fair value analysis leads to a potential market cap at this time of in between $1 and $2 billion.

The issue here is financing. The company has recently picked up $18.5 million in financing, thereby more than doubling the outstanding share count at price above and below the dollar. According to my estimations, the company has about two months worth of cash to continue operations at this time, and its latest financing included more than 5 million of warrants that were exercisable immediately at an exercise price of $0.85. Cash burn was $27.3 million in 2023, and with all trials ongoing, I do not see that going down in the foreseeable future. As a result, further financings and further dilution may be imminent.

There is an analyst target of $15 on the stock, representing a more than 1,000% upside. My analysis comes to the same conclusion. However, with the current financing and dilution concerns, it is unclear what lies ahead for investors in the near term.

All-in-all, the chances of success and the potential revaluation as a result outweigh the risks here in my eyes, which is why I am giving the stock a Buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cut But Not Broken: 3M's Rebound Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
Introduction

Historically speaking, the worst-performing stocks on the market are dividend cutters, including companies eliminating their dividend. This group of stocks turned a $100 investment in 1973 into $73. During this period, dividend growers turned this $100 bill into more than $14,000!

However, while the odds are against dividend cutters, they are not all bad!

One of them is the 3M Company (NYSE:MMM), which cut its quarterly dividend from $1.51 to $0.70 on May 14, turning the former Dividend King into a dividend cutter.

The good news is that 3M is one of the "good guys."

I started covering the company on September 29, 2023, again. Back then, I called it "A 30% Undervalued Dumpster Fire." Since then, shares have returned 75%, more than twice the return of the S&P 500 (SP500).

My most recent article on the stock was written on July 2, titled "3M: A Dividend Cutter With A Strong Buy Rating." The company has returned 32% since then.

With that said, I cannot blame investors who have sold the stock recently, as it took the company roughly six years before it bottomed and regained upside momentum. This has resulted in a ten-year total return of 71%, a mile below the market's 259% return.

Hence, the point of this article is to update my bullish thesis on 3M, as the company continues to prove it's one of the few cutters capable of returning to long-term dividend growth.

To make my case, I'm using its just-released earnings and other major developments that hint at more elevated capital gains.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let's get right to it!

The Recovery Continues

3M is not an easy company to assess. This has at least three reasons:

  • It is a company recovering from contracting earnings and lawsuits.
  • This year, it spun off its healthcare segment.
  • It is still a cyclical company operating in an environment of contracting manufacturing expectations (see below).

The company also has a new CFO, Anurag Maheshwari, who used to be the CFO of Otis Worldwide Corporation (OTIS). Mr. Maheshwari's first earnings call was off to a good start, as he reported 18% year-over-year non-GAAP earnings-per-share growth, supported by 1% organic revenue growth. When adjusted for geographic prioritization and product portfolio initiatives, organic growth rose by 2%.

Moreover, operating margins improved by 140 basis points to 23.0%, allowing the company to generate $1.5 billion in free cash flow, 141% of its net income.

However, in light of my cyclical headwind comments, the company saw mixed results, as it encountered volatile industrial demand, strength in electronics, lower automotive build rates, and softness in consumer retail.

  • Safety and Industrial organic growth: 0.9%. This segment saw growth in areas like roofing and electronics.
  • Transportation and Electronics organic growth: 2.0%. Here, electronic sales offset a global decline in car production.
  • Consumer business organic growth: -0.7%. Rising home improvement sales were unable to offset the general pressure on consumer sentiment in other areas.

Most of my more regular readers will know that all of this is in line with the macroeconomic discussions we had in prior comments.

Despite a challenging market for cyclical companies, 3M's ability to achieve positive organic growth by focusing on secular trends and improving margins through productivity gains and restructuring is a great sign.

The only thing better than strong earnings is updated guidance.

A Path To Sustained Elevated Growth

As we can see below, the company raised the lower end of its full-year earnings guidance by $0.20, now targeting an EPS range of $7.20 to $7.30.

On top of good 3Q24 results, the company is confident in its strategic goals for 2024. These goals are based on three pillars:

  • Driving sustained organic growth: This includes investing in innovation and improving commercial execution.
  • Improving its operational performance: The company is focused on efficiency improvements across the board. This is key to accelerating margin improvements.
  • Capital Deployment: Disciplined capital management is crucial to improve shareholder value. This includes winning back the trust of investors who jumped ship recently. Given how stubborn most investors are (and rightfully so!), this could take a long time.

As these strategic priorities sound extremely vague and generic (every company focuses on sustained organic growth, improving, margins, and deploying capital effectively, etc.), let me add some color.

For example, to accelerate growth, the company is focusing on R&D, including the introduction of more new products. This includes reducing administrative burdens on engineers and speeding up SKU setup time from 100 days to 60. In other words, products are getting to market faster and engineers can be more creative.

It is also shifting its focus, as it moved 100 people within R&D (and added 50 more researchers) to new product developments, especially in automotive, aerospace, electronics, and semiconductors.

According to the company, this realignment aims to reverse a decade-long decline in new product introductions, with a 10% increase in new launches expected this year.

Additionally, it is improving the way it reaches customers and deals with suppliers. After all, having better products is only helpful if customers are aware and able to get their products on time.

In addition to training new staff and improving its sales force and distribution network, it has done an impressive job in solving critical supply issues that have led to business losses in the past. I added emphasis to the quote below.

[...] we've seen continued progress in delivering on-time in full or OTIF to our customers. I know we've lost business and have paid fines due to poor delivery performance, and I'm encouraged by the steady improvement we're making, ending Q3 at 89% OTIF, up 5 points since the beginning of the year and 10 points above Q4 of 2022. - MMM 3Q24 Earnings Call.

The company also increased its supplier performance. For many years, the company has dealt with subdued on-time supplier delivery rates in the low 60% range. Thanks to measures like better demand prediction, reliability is now in the low 70% range.

While investors wait for 3M to further improve their business, they enjoy well-covered dividends and buybacks. Year-to-date, the company has generated $3.5 billion in free cash flow. That's after $1.7 billion in capital expenditures and R&D and 2% above its net income. This is a sign of high-quality earnings, as more than all of it is turned into distributable cash.

It also allowed the company to return $2.7 billion to shareholders. $1.1 billion of this was indirectly returned in the form of buybacks. The good news is that after the cut, MMM still yields 2.1% with a payout ratio of just 39%. That's based on the midpoint of its new 2024 guidance.

Going forward, I expect dividend growth to return.

MMM Stock Valuation

Using the FactSet data in the chart below, 3M is expected to grow its EPS by 8% and 6% in 2025 and 2026, respectively. These growth rates are favorable in light of macroeconomic challenges. If we were to see a cyclical growth bottom, I expect the actual numbers to be substantially higher.

That said, working with current numbers and its long-term average P/E ratio of 18.2x, we get a fair stock price target of $152, 13% above the current price.

This is slightly above the $149 target I gave in my prior article.

As a result, I remain bullish on 3M.

While the company is not turning into a fast-growing industrial juggernaut anytime soon, I believe it has what it takes to turn into a sustainable dividend grower again. This time, it will have a streamlined business model, better innovation, improved supply chains, and a valuable life lesson from the 2018-2023 downturn.

Personally, however, I am not buying 3M because of my elevated industrial exposure through railroads, machinery companies, and aerospace players. Currently, there isn't room for more (overlapping) exposure.

Takeaway

3M's journey from a struggling dividend cutter to a potential dividend grower is a lesson in resilience.

Despite significant setbacks, including legal challenges and a dividend cut, the company has made impressive improvements to its operations and capital efficiency.

Moreover, its better focus on innovation, supply chain improvements, and disciplined capital management is driving a slow but steady recovery.

While 3M isn't out of the woods yet, I believe its earnings growth potential and improved fundamentals make it a strong contender for long-term investors.

Hence, I am very confident that 3M will regain its place as a reliable dividend grower in the years ahead.

Pros & Cons

Pros:

  • Strong (And Important!) Recovery: 3M is bouncing back with impressive earnings growth and improving margins.
  • Innovation Focus: The company is accelerating product development, especially in high-growth sectors like automotive and electronics.
  • Free Cash Flow & Dividends: With strong free cash flow, 3M continues to reward shareholders through dividends and buybacks, even after the cut. However, it could take time until it wins back the trust of dividend investors again (see my cons).
  • Valuation Upside: At current levels, 3M remains undervalued, with a cyclical growth bottom being an additional wild card.

Cons:

  • Dividend Cutter Reputation: The dividend cut still hurts many, making it tough to regain investor trust.
  • Cyclical Exposure: 3M is exposed to economic cycles, which may slow its growth in tough markets.

data.title: Chevron Vs. XLE: The Energy Giant Is A Better Bet Than The ETF
data.description:
CVX and XLE: Previous thesis and oil price change

My last work on Chevron stock (NYSE:CVX) was published in early September 2024. That article was titled "Chevron Stock: Oversold And Undervalued With Prices Near 52-Week Low (Technical Analysis)." As you can already guess from the title, the focus of the article is on the technical indicators. In particular, I explained why the technical trading patterns show significant selling pressure judging by the price-volume information and its Relative Strength Index [RSI] but the business fundamentals remain sound.

Since that writing, there have been a few developments surrounding the stock that are worth updating. Some of these developments impact the entire energy sector as represented by the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLE), and some of the developments impact CVX more in particular. I will start with the leading factor in the former category: Oil price. Later in the article, I will address the factors that are more unique to CVX, such as the relocation of its headquarters and also its recent expansion. In the end, the takeaway is that we consider CVX an overall better package compared to the energy sector in terms of profitability, consistency, and growth potential. Yet CVX is for sale at a discounted price relative to XLE. As such, we recommend potential investors who are seeking exposure to the energy sector consider CVX first (which we rate as a Buy) before XLE (which we rate as a Hold).

With this, let me start with the oil price change. The chart below shows the performance of CVX and XLE in the past year (top panel) together with the Brent Crude Oil Spot Price (BCOSPNK). As seen, oil prices have been under large pressure recently and have declined by 21.38% in the past year alone. The compressed oil price in turn has pressured the performance of both XLE and CVX, but unevenly. The XLE fund has experienced a small total return of 3.18% in the past year, lagging the overall market by a large margin but leading CVX. As seen, Chevron has suffered a total loss of -5.66%. Such performance divergence is unwarranted in our view and has made CVX a far superior investment opportunity over XLE, as detailed next.

XLE ETF: Basis information

Before going into further details, a quick summary. XLE tracks a market cap weighted index of energy stocks (U.S. only) listed in the S&P 500. More specifically, according to the fund description:

XLE offers liquid exposure to a market-like basket of US energy firms, including companies in the oil, gas and consumable fuels, and energy equipment and services industries as identified by GICS. XLE pulls its stocks from the S&P 500 rather than the total market, so its portfolio mainly favors large caps. Holdings are weighted by market cap, subject to a capping methodology that ensures no single security exceeds 25% at each quarterly rebalance.

More specifically, the next table lists the top 10 holdings of XLE. As seen, Exxon Mobil (XOM) is the largest holding, representing 23.18% of the ETF's total assets. Chevron follows closely behind with a 15.41% stake. CVX represents one of the world’s largest integrated oil companies, with significant operation in most of the steps along the value chain in the industry. Its upstream operations include exploring for, producing, and transporting crude oil and natural gas and processing, liquefaction, etc. Its downstream operations include refining, marketing, and transportation of energy commodities and products.

XLE Vs. CVX: Why we prefer CVX

The table below presents a comparative analysis of CVX and XLE, focusing on valuation, profitability and dividend growth metrics. Note that all the inputs used in this table were gathered from Yahoo Finance, Seeking Alpha, and the fund’s homepage as of Oct. 21, 2024. To us, this comparison clearly shows XLE to be a "diworsification" for CVX. Overall, CVX offers a combination of strong profitability, a solid dividend growth track record, and better consistency, but yet is undervalued when compared to XLE.

Let me start with profitability. In terms of returns on equity, CVX's ROE is admittedly lower than XLE's as seen (by about 20%). However, I think ROE underestimates CVX’s return on its investments, and ROCE (return on capital employed) is a better measure here. As detailed in an earlier article:

For businesses like CVX, I consider the following items capital actually employed 1) Working capital, including payables, receivables, inventory, 2) Gross Property, Plant, and Equipment, and 3) Research and development expenses (a relatively minor expense for a business like CVX) are also amortized and considered part of its capital employed.

Under these considerations, CVX’s ROCE is currently around 23% based on my estimate of its 2024 FY financials, far better than the sector’s 16.5%. Note that for the sector, I use the ROE to approximate its ROCE. For a sizable group of diverse stocks, the differences between ROCE and ROE tend to average out and thus become numerically close.

In terms of valuation, based on the price-to-cash flow ratio, CVX is currently trading at a premium relative to XLE (by about 6% as seen). However, in terms of P/E and P/B ratios, CVX's multiple are lower than XLE. To wit, its P/E ratio is lower than XLE by about 3%, and the P/B ratio lower than XLE by about 19%, suggesting noticeable undervaluation.

Although given CVX’s dividend champion status and the significant role of dividend payouts in the energy sector, we think dividend yields offer more insights into their valuation comparison, as detailed next.

XLE and CVX: Dividend yield in focus

Even though both CVX and XLE have maintained a consistent dividend growth rate of 6.4% in the past five years, CVX's longer track record of dividend increases offers far better consistency. As a dividend champ, CVX has a significant advantage with 36 consecutive years of dividend growth compared to XLE.

More importantly, the current yield spread [YD] between CVX and XLE sends a strong signal about CVX’s attractive valuation. The concept of YD and my method for computing it has been provided in our earlier article. Here I will only quote the gist:

  • Dividends provide a backdoor to quickly estimate the owners’ earnings. Dividends are the most reliable financial information and the least open to interpretation. In investing, we always prefer a simpler method that relies on fewer and unambiguous data points rather than a more complicated method that depends on more ambiguous data points.
  • The dividend yield spread [YS] is based on a timeless intuition. No matter how times change, for two securities that pay regular dividends, the yield spread between them always measures their risk premium relative to each other. A large spread provides a higher margin of safety and vice versa.

Against this background, the next chart illustrates the historical YS between CVX and XLE in the past 10 years, between 2014 and 2024.

As seen, throughout the period, CVX's dividend yield has been above XLE, as reflected by the long-term average YS of 0.211% as seen. The key observations in my mind are twofold.

First, under current conditions, CVX is trading at a spread far above the historical average (0.939% vs 0.211%). This spread is not only far above the historical average but also near the thickest level in the past 10 years as seen, indicating one of the largest relative valuation discounts to the sector.

Second, you can also see that the YS has exhibited significant volatility in the past and this volatility is primarily caused by XLE, not CVX. Notably, as you can see from the chart above, during the pandemic, the YS dropped to a bottom of near -8%. This is largely due to the extreme price correction that XLE has suffered (more than 50% off the peak of its pre-pandemic level as you can see from the chart below), which in turn caused a large yield surge. In contrast, CVX suffered far less correction as seen. The fact that a single stock can show far better strength during a major recession than an ETF serves as another key indicator of its resilience.

Other risks and final thoughts

In terms of downside risks, CVX and XLE share many common risks. The primary common risks facing the oil and gas industry include fluctuations in oil prices, regulatory changes, and geopolitical risks. Oil prices are inherently volatile, influenced by geopolitical events, economic conditions, and supply-demand dynamics. Regulatory changes, such as environmental regulations or tax policies, can significantly impact the industry's profitability.

For CVX, there are some additional risks that can impact it in more specific ways. CVX has been expanding its footprint globally. It has recently executed agreements in Namibia, Brazil, Equatorial Guinea, and Angola to increase its global exploration acreage position. Such expansion provides growth opportunities but also heightens its exposure to geopolitical risks. Domestically, the company is in the process of a $53-billion acquisition of Hess. The Federal Trade Commission has recently completed an antitrust review of the acquisition. The acquisition and the subsequent synergy (assuming the deal will be completed) could take some time and face some uncertainties, especially considering that part of the deal is that CEO John Hess will not be appointed to the CVX board after the acquisition. Recently, the company has also announced a relocation of its headquarters from San Ramon, Calif., to Houston. I expect this relocation to enable better collaboration and engagement among CVX’s executives, employees, and key business partners in the long run. However, in the near term, this move might increase costs and temporarily reduce efficiency.

All told, I believe CVX is a far more attractive investment than the average energy sector represented by XLE for several reasons. In particular, I consider XLE to be a diworsification of CVX under current conditions. I always prefer a single and well-managed company (or a few of them) with a strong track profitability record and reasonable overvaluation over diversification across multiple companies. This article compares CVX to XLE in terms of their financial metrics, valuation, profitability, and consistency. To me, this comparison clearly demonstrates CVX to be a better-rounded investment package compared to XLE.

data.title: Kingsoft Corporation: Positive Short-Term And Long-Term Prospects (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I raised my investment rating for Kingsoft Corporation Limited (OTCPK:KSFTF) [3888:HK] from a Hold to a Buy. The near-term outlook for the company's online games segment has been boosted by the introduction of a new mobile game known as JX3 Ultimate, while its office software business is well-positioned to take advantage of China's increased focus on domestic or local software. I have become bullish on KSFTF considering the company's favorable prospects.

The earlier May 29, 2024 update was focused on the evaluation of KSFTF's financial results for the first quarter of this year.

Kingsoft Corporation's shares can be traded on the Hong Kong equity market and the Over-The-Counter market. The company's shares listed on the Stock Exchange of Hong Kong are very liquid with a mean daily trading value of around $25 million for the past three months as per S&P Capital IQ data. On the other hand, Kingsoft Corporation's OTC shares have poor trading liquidity. US stockbrokers like Interactive Brokers offer trading services for Hong Kong-listed shares.

New Mobile Game Is Expected To Be Near-Term Growth Driver

KSFTF noted in the company's 2024 interim report released late last month that "the mobile version" of its PC game JX3 Online known as "JX3 Ultimate was launched successfully in June." This is a significant development that is anticipated to boost the performance of Kingsoft Corporation's online games business segment in the near term.

Canadian gaming website wtfast describes JX3 Online as "a 3D martial-arts themed MMORPG (Massively Multiplayer Online Role-Playing Game) that features ancient Chinese swordsman legends." KSFTF's JX3 Online PC game was first introduced to the market 15 years ago, having boasted a few million users on a daily basis in 2020. With my previous July 19, 2021 write-up, I emphasized that "there is a compelling need for Kingsoft Corporation to launch new games" to "reduce its reliance on" its "flagship PC game" JX Online III or JX3 Online.

Kingsoft Corporation's new JX3 Ultimate mobile game launch is the right move to leverage on the popularity of JX3 Online to seek out new gaming revenue sources and diversify its games portfolio.

The new JX3 Ultimate mobile game is likely to have performed well, taking into account recent disclosures.

The producer in charge of the JX3 Ultimate did an interview (translated using Google) with Chinese media platform Sohu in August this year, which revealed key statistics pertaining to KSFTF's new mobile game. The number of monthly active users for JX3 Ultimate had surpassed the 10 million mark in the first month following its introduction to the market. There are two positive surprises worth highlighting. One surprise is that approximately 60% of JX3 Ultimate's monthly active users were JX3 Online's dormant players, and the majority of the dormant players which returned to JX3 Ultimate haven't played a JX3 franchise game for the past seven years. The other surprise is that the average age of new players (who have never played any JX3 franchise game previously) for JX3 Ultimate was 22 years, or much lower than JX3 Online's 26 years average player age.

There are three key takeaways from JX3 Ultimate's metrics disclosed by the game's producer in the recent interview cited above. Firstly, the JX3 Ultimate mobile game is popular, considering the monthly active user metric exceeding 10 million. Secondly, the new mobile game has rekindled interest in the JX3 gaming franchise, with a meaningful number of dormant players coming back to play JX3 Ultimate. Thirdly, KSFTF has managed to attract younger new gamers with this new mobile game offering.

In its 2024 interim report, Kingsoft Corporation highlighted that JX3 Ultimate's "cross-platform data inheritance and interconnectivity with the flagship (PC game) JX3 Online" drove "a new record in daily active users across all platforms" for the JX3 gaming franchise in the second quarter. This sends a clear signal that JX3 Ultimate has had a positive impact on the company's online games business.

KSFTF had raised the full-year FY 2024 revenue growth guidance for its online games business segment from +10% to +20% according to an August 22, 2024 research report (not publicly available) titled "Q2 Review: Upward Revision In Games Guidance" issued by Soochow Securities. As a reference, Kingsoft Corporation's online games business registered a +10.4% YoY increase in segment revenue to RMB 2,198 million for 1H 2024, which accounted for 47.7% of its 2024 interim top line.

Kingsoft Corporation's short-term outlook is favorable. KSFTF's new JX3 Ultimate mobile game has been a success, and this makes it more likely that the company's online games segment will meet its +20% segment revenue expansion target for the current year.

Office Software Business Has Good Long-Term Growth Potential

Kingsoft Corporation's other key business segment, representing 52.3% of the company's 1H 2024 total revenue, is its office software business. The office software segment's office software branded as WPS Office is a potential substitute for the software programs offered by Microsoft (MSFT) Office.

A recent October 18, 2024 South China Morning Post news report drew attention to China President Xi Jinping's comments indicating that the country "must accelerate the realization of high-level technological self-reliance." China's stance on increasing its dependence on domestic technology solutions providers is a tailwind for Chinese company KSFTF's office software business.

As per Reuters' October 2023 news article, "China spent 1.4 trillion yuan replacing foreign hardware and software in 2022" which was +16% more than what the country spent on this similar endeavor in the previous year. Reuters subsequently published another report in April this year, highlighting that China's "state-owned enterprises were instructed in 2022 to replace office software systems with domestic products by 2027." It is apparent that China is determined to diversify away from foreign software products in the coming years, as per these recent media reports.

Chinese research firm CCID Consulting published a research report (translated using Google) titled "2022-2023 China's ICT Ecosystem And PC market" last year. There are approximately 30 million existing PCs used by the Chinese government and the ministries, with new yearly PC purchases of around 5 million units based on CCID Consulting's estimates outlined in its 2023 research report.

Kingsoft Corporation's WPS Office software is dominant in its home market, with a 90% share of the office software market in China. This means that KSFTF's office software business is a major beneficiary of the growing demand for domestic office software in the Chinese market.

The segment revenue for KSFTF's office software business rose by +17.1% to RMB 4,557 million in the most recent fiscal year or FY 2023. Kingsoft Corporation's annual office software revenue growth could potentially accelerate to the low-to-mid-twenties percentage range in the medium-to-long term, riding on policy tailwinds. Assuming that the Chinese government continues to replace old PCs or buy new PCs at a rate of 5 million units every year going forward as per CCID Consulting's estimates, this translates into incremental office software revenue of over RMB 1 billion for China's office software industry and KSFTF (90% market share). An annual individual subscription plan for Kingsoft Corporation's WPS Office costs as much as RMB 240, which I have used as a reference for the estimated corporate subscription plan pricing.

The growth prospects for KSFTF's office software segment are excellent. Kingsoft Corporation's office software business is a leading domestic player in the Chinese office software market, which is benefiting from the country's emphasis on self-dependence relating to technology.

Key Risks

There are two major risk factors concerning Kingsoft Corporation.

The first risk to consider is the popularity of KSFTF's new JX3 Ultimate mobile game. If Kingsoft Corporation fails to keep JX3 Ultimate's users engaged, this game might witness a significantly slower pace of user growth in the coming months.

The second risk to watch is a change in the Chinese government's policy or stance on technology adoption. Assuming that China decides to move slower with the transition to domestic or local software, KSFTF's office software business could possibly underperform in the future.

Bottom Line

I have a positive view of the short-term prospects for Kingsoft Corporation's online games business and the long-term outlook for its office software business. KSFTF's appealing valuations based on a comparison of its earnings multiple and earnings growth rate also support my Buy rating. The market currently values Kingsoft Corporation at 21 times consensus next twelve months' normalized P/E, while the company's FY 2023-2027 consensus EPS CAGR is +51%. This translates into a very enticing PEG (Price-to-Earnings Growth) ratio of around 0.41 times, as per S&P Capital IQ data.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The 10-Year Treasury Rate May Rise Above 6%
data.description:
The United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) is at a crossroads and may have much further to climb. Despite the Federal Reserve cutting rates, economic resilience is pushing interest rates higher. If history is any guide, the 10-year rate could surge to 6% or beyond, driven by a stronger-than-expected economy, persistent inflation, a steepening yield curve, and increased US spending.

Given the recent run of strong economic data, it may be surprising that the Fed cut the Fed Funds rate by 50 bps in September because of a cooling labor market and inflation. However, the September jobs report showed better-than-expected revisions for July and August and depicted unexpected economic strength. Meanwhile, the September CPI was hotter than expected.

Steeper Yield Curve

Due to the robust data and Fed rate cuts, the yield curve is steepening as the front of the curve declines, expecting more Fed rate cuts, while the back of the curve rises due to better-than-expected growth. If the Fed and the Fed Fund Futures are correct, and the neutral rate is around 3 to 3.5%, then the 10-year rate is probably too low based on historical norms.

The 10-year rate is still trading 62 bps below the Fed Funds rate. That spread will likely rise if the Fed continues on its rate-cutting path, as indicated and based on current Fed Fund futures.

Currently, the Fed Fund futures see the overnight rate bottoming around 3.4% by December 2025. This would imply that the economy's neutral rate is somewhere in that region.

Going back to the 1960s, the 10-year tends to peak between 275 and 385 bps above the Fed Funds rate. If the neutral rate is indeed somewhere around 3 to 3.5%, it would mean, at a minimum, the 10-year rate rises to a range of 5.75% to 7.35%

Typically, the yield curve peaks when the Fed Funds rate falls and moves away from the 10-year. Therefore, at the current rate of around 4.20%, the yield curve would peak at most at just 120 bps. But the risk is that because we are coming out of a period where rates have been suppressed for so long, it is possible that the 10-year Treasury hasn't found its natural rate. Still, the chances of the 10-year rate falling as the Fed cut is unlikely unless economic growth slows or inflation falls below 2%.

2% Inflation

Since 1960, the US 10-year has tended to trade about 1.95% above the inflation rate, using the median. This would suggest that the 10-year rate probably has a bit more to rise from the current spread of 1.8%. But more importantly, this gives us a sense that if inflation remains around the Fed's target of 2%, then the 10-year rate should find a floor around the 4% region, provided there is no recession or inflation doesn't fall below 2%.

So again, if the yield curve continues to normalize and looks like it has in the past, it tells us that the 10-year rate isn't likely to see too much downside. Therefore, the risk is that the back-of-the-curve continues to push higher as part of the normalization process.

Term-Premium

Another factor that is difficult to account for is the US debt and deficit spending and whether the term premium in the 10-year rises or falls. Investors may simply demand a higher yield if the debt continues to mount and the spending deficit widens. Today's term premium is only 12 bps, which is very low by historical standards. Again, it is hard to say where the term premium will be in the future, but the risk, based on the state of US spending, is that the term premium goes higher, pushing 10-year rates higher.

Impacts On ETFs

The 10-year rate is likely to be determined by the neutral rate, the term premium, and the health of the US economy. But, at least based on the present data, the 10-year rate has much more risk of upside than downside. This would be bearish for ETFs such as the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) or the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF). These focus on the longer end of the curve and would see the price of the ETF decline as the rate rises.

The next few months will be important in determining the 10-year rate's direction, and given how difficult previous months have been to navigate, it will probably be tough to assess the current state of the economy.

All one can do is remain vigilant and open-minded.
